We should not think that searching the word or works of God is useless just because we cannot explain all difficulties. We can learn many things that are good for ourselves and useful to others. But people cannot always decide who are objects of God's special love, or under his wrath; and God will certainly put a difference between what is valuable and what is worthless in the next world. The difference as to present happiness comes from the inner support and comfort the righteous enjoy, and the benefit they receive from varied trials and mercies. As far as people are left to themselves, their hearts are full of evil; and success in sin causes them even to defy God by daring wickedness. Though, before death, the righteous and the wicked may often seem to fare alike, there will be a great difference between them afterward.

The state of the most despicable living person is preferable to that of the most noble who have died without repentance. Solomon encourages the wise and pious to cheerful confidence in God, whatever their condition in life. The smallest portion, coming from their Father's love, in answer to prayer, will have a special flavor. Not that we should set our hearts upon the delights of sense, but what God has given us we may use with wisdom. The joy here described is the gladness of heart that springs from a sense of God’s favor. This is the world of service; that which is to come is the world of reward. All in their positions can find some work to do. And above all, sinners have the salvation of their souls to seek after, believers have to prove their faith, adorn the gospel, glorify God, and serve their generation.

People's success seldom equals their expectations. We must use means, but not trust in them: if we succeed, we must give God the praise; if we are disappointed, we must submit to his will. Those who put off the important concerns of their souls are caught in Satan’s net, which he tempts with some worldly object, for which they reject or neglect the gospel, and go on in sin until they suddenly fall into destruction.

A person may, by wisdom, bring about what he could never do by strength. If God is for us, who can be against us, or stand before us? Solomon observes the power of wisdom, even though it may face outward disadvantages. How powerful are right words! But wise and good people must often content themselves with the satisfaction of having done good, or at least, tried to do it, when they cannot do the good they would, nor have the praise they should. How many of the good gifts, both of nature and Providence, does one sinner destroy and waste! He who destroys his own soul destroys much good. One sinner may draw many into his destructive ways. See who are the friends and enemies of a kingdom or a family; if one righteous person does much good, and one sinner destroys much good.
